serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of milk is one glass (approximately 244 g or 250 ml), providing about 163 kcal, 8.3 g protein, 8.6 g fat, and 12.6 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1097512); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 67 kcal, protein 3.39 g, fat 3.53 g, carbohydrate 5.15 g) taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as provided in the input record (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, vitamins cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole milk (FDC ID 1097512), which is consistent with HPB values. All values are per 100 g.

gi_source.title: Milk, full-fat, GI value 31 — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 31 for full-fat cow's milk is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values. Glycemic load per 100 g basis calculated as GL = GI × carb_g / 100 = 31 × 5.15 / 100 = 1.6, consistent with the input record.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (48 mg/100 g), high calcium (123 mg/100 g), and potassium (~150 mg/100 g) support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Plain whole milk is suitable for people with hypertension due to its low sodium content and vasodilatory minerals; choose low-fat or skimmed variants to reduce saturated fat intake, and avoid flavoured or sweetened milk products that add sodium and sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 31) with naturally occurring lactose (4.81 g/100 g sugar); protein and fat slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole milk has a low GI of 31 and is appropriate for people with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions; choose plain unsweetened milk, monitor total carbohydrate load from larger servings, and avoid flavoured milk with added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~2.28 g/100 g) is moderate; calcium and potassium are cardioprotective, but high intake of full-fat dairy may raise LDL cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Whole milk can be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate amounts; individuals with cardiovascular disease or elevated LDL cholesterol should consider switching to low-fat or skimmed milk to reduce saturated fat intake while retaining calcium and protein benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (123 mg/100 g) and protein (3.39 g/100 g) support bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids; saturated fat (2.28 g/100 g) is well below the 5 g/100 g concern threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Milk is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients requiring bone protection; choose low-fat milk to minimise saturated fat, and ensure adequate vitamin D from supplementary sources as milk alone may be insufficient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milk is a nutrient-dense, opaque white liquid food secreted by the mammary glands of cows (Bos taurus) and is one of the most universally consumed dairy products across all cultures and age groups. Fresh whole cow's milk has a smooth, slightly viscous texture and a mild, gently sweet flavour derived from its naturally occurring lactose content, with a clean, faintly creamy finish on the palate. It is typically consumed chilled as a standalone beverage, warmed as a base for hot drinks such as coffee, tea, Milo, or hot chocolate, and is a foundational ingredient in an enormous range of culinary preparations including baking (cakes, breads, pancakes), savoury cooking (sauces, soups, curries), and the production of secondary dairy products such as cheese, yogurt, butter, cream, and ice cream. In Singapore and across Southeast Asia, milk is commonly consumed as a breakfast drink, mixed into kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea), and used in traditional desserts such as sago pudding and cendol. Commercially, milk is almost universally pasteurized — heated briefly to eliminate pathogenic bacteria — and homogenized to prevent cream separation, resulting in a shelf-stable product with consistent texture. Per 100 g, whole cow's milk provides approximately 67 kcal of energy, 3.39 g of complete protein containing all essential amino acids (including casein and whey fractions), 3.53 g of total fat (of which 2.28 g is saturated fat), and 5.15 g of carbohydrates (4.81 g as lactose, the primary milk sugar). It is an excellent dietary source of calcium (123 mg/100 g, approximately 12% of the adult daily requirement), vitamin B12 (0.45 mcg), riboflavin (0.17 mg), phosphorus (95 mg), potassium (150 mg), and vitamin D (1.3 mcg). Dietary fibre is naturally zero as milk is an animal-derived liquid food. Clinically, milk has a low glycemic index of 31, attributed to the combined effect of protein, fat, and the slower digestion of lactose compared to free glucose, making it appropriate for people with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions. Its low sodium content (48 mg/100 g) and high calcium and potassium levels support blood pressure management in hypertension. For cardiovascular health, low-fat or skimmed variants are often preferred to reduce saturated fat intake. Milk is contraindicated for individuals with lactose intolerance or cow's milk protein allergy (CMPA), and those with these conditions should seek lactose-free or plant-based alternatives.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (confidence 0.95 per input record). Secondary nutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1097512) for whole milk, which is an approved primary source. GI value of 31 is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All values are consistent across sources.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g: milk is an animal-derived liquid food and naturally contains no dietary fibre — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central. Iron is 0.0 mg/100 g: milk is a naturally poor source of iron; the value of 0.0 is confirmed per HPB Singapore database (trace amounts below detection threshold). Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100 g: raw milk contains negligible vitamin C; pasteurization further reduces any residual amount to effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central. Saturated fat (2.28 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.81 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.12 g/100 g), trans fat (0.07 g/100 g), cholesterol (14 mg/100 g), and all vitamin and mineral values not present in the HPB input record are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 1097512 (whole milk, 3.25% milkfat), which is consistent with the HPB macro profile. Glycemic load of 1.6 is reported on a per-100 g basis (GL = 31 × 5.15 / 100 = 1.6). The input record listed 'Embedded GI Database' as the GI source; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) as the approved GI source per Project Veritas source policy. Local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided as milk is a universally consumed food in Singapore and these names support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore context.
